channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Perhaps You’re Not Yourself, But Her
item.description: German director Mascha Schilinski’s visually evocative 2025 film suggests the influence of Francesca Woodman’s photographic work.

item.title: Cold War Reborn
item.description: The Arctic may well be the center of the next global conflict.

item.title: Seeing Like a Worm
item.description: Jessica Riskin’s Lamarckian (history of) science is a remarkably transformative work.

item.title: Mortal Urges
item.description: Three recent translations of Milo De Angelis, Jean Follain, and Vidyā will fill their reader with welcome weirdness.

item.title: The Artist Who Knew Almost Everyone Important and Made Pictures of Them
item.description: Bryan Garner’s new book revisits the career of little-known artist Oskar Stoessel.

item.title: Sadism as Psychotherapy
item.description: Jon Stock’s recent book examines the deplorable career of prominent psychiatrist Willam Sargant and his brand of bio-therapeutics.

item.title: Strange Old Alien
item.description: The Monochrome Set in their fifth decade of music.

item.title: Vulgar Marxism, or Stop Me If You’ve Heard This One Before
item.description: A diagnosis of our stale culture sounds very familiar.

item.title: A Simulation of Worship
item.description: In Wayne Koestenbaum’s new novel, the quest to understand the loved object’s inner life takes on a Talmudic quality.

item.title: Digging Through the Debris
item.description: Theodor Adorno’s early music criticism, newly translated to English, showcases his expressionist, concrete style of examination, which grew increasingly moralizing after the war.

item.title: Facing the Past
item.description: Ben Lerner’s new novel is a portrait of an artist within the world, trying to live a life in spite of and toward language.

item.title: Fantasy Dismantled
item.description: Saïd Khatibi’s ‘The End of the Sahara,’ the first Algerian crime novel translated from Arabic by Bitter Lemon Press, potently critiques Orientalist fantasies of desert romance.

item.title: The Structures That Make Violence Legible
item.description: Tom McCarthy’s critical study of postwar Austrian writer Ingeborg Bachmann shows a deep sensitivity to form.
